title:
@_ashleypeacock: The use cases for Dynamic Workers seem infinite

What if you could validate ever...
rawContent:
Tweet by @_ashleypeacock (Ashley Peacock)
Author bio: Cloudflare Dev Expert & Author 📚 Cloudflare book: https://t.co/g0o7YoD7ae Substack: https://t.co/dRGN048aY9
Text: The use cases for Dynamic Workers seem infinite

What if you could validate every PR released to production by having smoke tests run in Dynamic Workers?

Things don't always go to plan when releasing new code to production, but verifying every deploy worked as intended is quite difficult. You can ingest logs and traces, check SLOs etc. but that just seeks to prove nothing is significantly broken. What if the new button released doesn't actually trigger the intended API call? Or what if there's a UI regression?

Those will likely go unnoticed until either you manually verify, or a user reports it to you. In a world where running untrusted code is now effortless inside of a Dynamic Worker, I built a small app that uses the Agents SDK, Browser Rendering, Workers AI and Dynamic Workers to run smoke tests to verify behavior

In the real world, you'd have this trigger automatically after a deployment and give the AI agent JIRA cards, the pull request and so forth. The AI agent then creates a series of scenarios and test plans, and sets about executing it using a headless browser via Browser Rendering

All the code it generates is run safely in a Dynamic Worker that prevents outbound internet access, and the Dynamic Worker accesses resources it needs, such as Browser Rendering, via Workers RPC

The more I use Dynamic Workers, the more I see the power of them

I think these lightweight sandboxes are going to be the future of how we run the majority of AI-generated code in production environments. They are just so damn fast and easy to use, and the majority of the time, you don't need file system access or a full OS anyway
